Assumption_Description: SDMs often assume that species are in equilibrium (or quasi-equilibrium) with their environment in their native range. That is, suitable habitat is fully occupied or saturated (e.g. Gallien et al. 2012, Zurell et al. 2024).
Violation_of_Assumptions: This assumption can be violated for several reasons. For instance, the native range of the species can be restricted by biotic interactions (e.g. competition, predation, disturbances, etc.) (Richmond et al. 2010). There is also increasing evidence that range-shifting species are lagging behind their climatically suitable habitat (Svenning et al. 2008), leading to suitable habitat not yet colonised (“colonisation credit”) .... Zurell et al. 2023, see also Thuiller et al. 2019, Foden et al 2007; Foster et al. 2022).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Underprediciton of potential regions of suitability, Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range expansion
Potential_Solutions: Use historical range information for model training., Develop a dispersal model to best estimate the species potential distribution (Merow et al. 2016)., Include species' ecological traits in SDMs (Hanspach et al. 2010; Jiguet et al. 2010; Viana & Chase 2022)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption., Can we use disequilibrium indicators (e.g. filled vs. unfilled regional niches) to predict a species' future spread or adaptation potential?., "Use models that incorporate biological processes (e.g. thermal limits, energetics) rather than just statistical correlations, as these better capture real-world limitations beyond simple climate.", "Add functions relating occurrence likelihood to distance from known locations to account for dispersal limitations and mass effects, improving niche models.", Implement Nested SDMs (N-SDMs; Guisan et al. 2025).
Questions_General: "Does the species' observed range reflect its true potential (realized niche) or is lagging due to dispersal, time lags, or biotic interactions?", "How does the equilibrium status differ for invasive species (rapid spread, niche filling) versus native species, and how does this affect risk assessment?"

Assumption_Description: SDMs typically assume that species maintain their ecological niche (the set of environmental conditions they require to thrive) across space and time. That is, "Ecological niche models also assume niche conservatism" (Wiens et al. 2009, Gallien et al. 2012).
Violation_of_Assumptions: This assumption can be violated for several reasons. For instance, evolutionary or behavioral adaptation to environmental conditions. New ecological relationships in introduce range. "Short-generation species are capable of rapid evolutionary change (Franks et al. 2007)." (Wiens et al. 2009, see also (Root et al. 2003).
Effects_Subcategory: Reduced model transferability, Biased estimates of range dynamics, Failure to capture rapid adaptation
Potential_Solutions: Shorten timescale of analysis., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "Is the niche truly static, or can it evolve or shift?"

Assumption_Description: SDMs assummes that species occur at all locations where habitat is suitable and can readily move to new suitable areas if they are available (e.g.  Jeschke & Strayer 2008). SDMs implicitly assume that species have had enough time to disperse to all suitable habitats within the study area and are at equilibrium with their environment (no dispersal limitation). This is a convenience assumption used to simplify the modeling process.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Modelers usually consider two extreme scenarios: full dispersal scenarios vs. no-dispersal scenarios. See also Zurell et al. 2023)
Effects_Subcategory: Over-prediction of a species' potential range, Overestimation of suitable habitat, Reduced model transferability, Understimate range contraction
Potential_Solutions: Incorporating distance constraints into species distribution models (Allouche et al. 2008)., "More advanced, mechanistic, or dynamic SDMs (D-SDMs) often incorporate dispersal as a key variable to simulate how species ranges might expand or shift over time, especially in response to climate change (e.g.  Shipley et al. 2021; Zurell 2017; Evans et al 2015).", "Modelers might consider this criterion is irrelevant or has minimum impact on the accuracy of model precictions. Modelers are looking only for high model accuracy and low uncertainty, regarless of explaining of predictors."
Questions_General: Is the species' distribution potentially limited by dispersal?, Can incorporating distance or connectivity constraints improve model predictions?, What is the relative importance of dispersal versus environmental factors in shaping distributions at different spatial scales?

Assumption_Description: "It is usually assume that biotic interations only introduce statistical "noise" and don't cause significant biases in the species' distribution within the study area." (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024). . See also for Biotic importance (Araújo & Luoto 2007; Fern et al 2019) and species interactions (Suttle et al. 2007, Poggiato et al. 2025). See also Co-occurrence is not evidence of ecological interactions (Blanchet et al. 2020) and others (Jeschke & Strayer 2008).
Violation_of_Assumptions: This assumption can be violated for several reasons. For instance, there could be a prominent role for species interactions in ecosystem response to climate change. "The influence of biotic interactions on species distributions is still debated, and potential inaccuracies in data sources (e.g. metawebs, environmental covariates) could mean that including prey as additional predictors may not always enhance predictions and may sometimes degrade them." (Poggiato et al. 2025; Godsoe & Harmon 2012)
Effects_Subcategory: Inability to capture the true mechanisms driving species distributions, Potentially reduce model performance
Potential_Solutions: Include biotic interactions as predictors (Heikkinen et al. 2007)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: To what degree are you concerned about how well important biological assumptions are explained and justified?
Questions_General: How do biotic factors might affect species distribution and abundances?, Is it acceptable to ignore biotic interactions ath the scale of the analysis?, Do biotic factors improve model performance?, "Which types of interactions matter most?  Should models prioritize negative interactions like competition or predation, or positive ones like mutualism?"

Assumption_Description: SDMs usually assumes that genetic and phenotypic composition of species is constant over space and time." (Jeschke & Strayer 2008).
Violation_of_Assumptions: This assumption can be violated for several reasons. For instance, genetic or phenotypic changes might occur when species confront new environmental conditions. Evolutionary change does happen and sometimes even relatively fast. (Jeschke & Strayer 2008). Se also Fitzpatrick & Keller (2014), Kebede et al. (2021), Hällfors et al. (2015).
Effects_Subcategory: Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Ignoring the potential for adaptation, Incorrect range predicitions
Potential_Solutions: Use trait data sets as a proxy of population phenotypic differentiation (Kebede et al. 2021; Chardon et al. 2019)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: Do SDMs accurately capture local adaptation?, Can models predict the potential for evolutionary rescue?

Criterion: Understanding the ecological processes driving species distribution and abundances
Assumption_Description: Model resemble full understanding of the ecological processes driving species distributions/abundances acting at multiple spatial and temporal scales (e.g. Elith & Leathwick 2009; Austin 2009; Bryn et al. 2021; Ulrich et al. 2022), such as environmental filtering, biotic interactions, dispersal, ecological drift, etc.  Predictors do not influence species one by one, but act on the species in concert. A few major complex-gradients normally account for most of the variation in species composition that can be explained environmentally. Species occur within a restricted interval along each major complex-gradient (e.g. Halvorsen 2012).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Lack of knowledge on the true relationships between variables for field data and the shape of these relationships. There is limited knowledge of the species distribution dynamics (e.g. colonization and local extinction) and these dynamics might not be included in the model.  See Vallecillo et al. 2019, Howard et al. 2020).
Effects_Subcategory: Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Inability to Disentangle Mechanisms, Incorrect range predicitions
Potential_Solutions: Include disturbance processes (i.e. fire regimes) as potential predictors of changes in species distributions (e.g.; Vallecillo et al. 2009)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption., "Modelers might consider this criterion is irrelevant or has minimum impact on the accuracy of model precictions. Modelers are looking only for high model accuracy and low uncertainty, regarless of explaining of predictors."
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: To what degree are you concerned about how well important biological assumptions are explained and justified?

Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: "Input data are free of substantial errors regarding taxonomic identification" (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024, see also Aubry et al. 2017, Maldonado et al. 2015, Araujo et al. 2019).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Older records might be prone to suffer from misidentification
Effects_Subcategory: Understimate range expansion, Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range contraction, Distortions or inflations of inferred environmental associations
Potential_Solutions: Implement partial vetting (Soley et al. 2024)., Standardize and clean data for given taxonomic groups and regions (Soley et al. 2024)., Remove occurrences without spatial reference., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "Are the species taxonomic identities are well understood, stable, and consistently applied?"

Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: Input data are free of substantial errors regarding georeference (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024; Araujo et al. (2019). See also Aubry et al. 2017, Maldonado et al. 2015 and positional error see Gábor et al. 2019). Increaisng sample size does not overcome the effect of positional error (Gábor et al. 2019)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Errors in georeferencing, Older records might be prone to suffer from uncertainty in spatial georeferencing
Effects_Subcategory: Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Erros in model intepretation, Misleading Results for Specialist Species
Potential_Solutions: Implement partial vetting (Soley et al. 2024)., Remove occurrences without spatial reference., Standardize and clean data for given taxonomic groups and regions (Soley et al. 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: Do all species react the same way to positional errors?, Can the negative effects of positional error be mitigated by adjusting the spatial resolution (grain size)?, Is the modeling technique robust to handle positional errors?, "Is the effect of positional uncertainty modulated by the spatial structure of environmental predictors? (e.g. abrupt enevironemntal changes are more sensitive to positional errors)", Does increasing sample size (number of occurrence points) mitigate the negative influence of positional error?, "Does the type of positional error (e.g. random noise vs. systematic bias near roads/cities) matter?", "How can positional uncertainty be quantified and visualized in model outputs? (e.g. maps of ignorance).", How does positional error interact with other data quality issues?

Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: The aasumption is that the spatial and temporal uncertainty are smaller than the resolution of the environmental predictors (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024; https://www.science.org/doi/10.1126/sciadv.aat4858). See also Maldonado et al. 2015 and Seo et al. 2008.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Mismatches in spatiotemporal scales between species and covariates datasets (e.g. Wiens et al. 2010).
Effects_Subcategory: At a very fine resolution (grain) distributions may not match environmental factors closely because behavioral interactions (territoriality social attraction) may override habitat selection, At the coarse resolution of a broad geographic range only the most general environmental relations may emerge in correlational analyses, Overestimation of suitable habitat
Potential_Solutions: Assess apropiate scale of the analyisis (matching with covariates)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: How concerned are you about the temporal distribution of detections?, "If so, explain. What is an appropriate truncation value (i.e. minimum year)."
Questions_General: Is the spatial accuracy of the distributional occurrence data matches the spatial resolution of the environmental predictor variables?

Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: "Sampling effort has been homogeneous across the study region, so that no bias exists in the conditions inferred to represent the species' environmental associations" (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024); "Sampled via systematically designed surveys demonstrated to encompass the major environmental gradients occupied by the taxon, and spatial extent of the taxon’s occurrence, within the study area" (Araujo et al. 2019). Unbiased species data refers to a non-random distribution of presence data (also known as spacies data clustering). HSMs attempt to quantify the environmental niche of species through models, and therefore the data used to do so need to include all possible environments that represent suitable habitats for the species modeled (Guisan et al. 2017). See also: Dubos et al. (2022), Taylor et al. (2019), Vollering et al. (2019).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Bias is likely to arise when the chosen sampling design lacks a random component or when the data are gathered without employing a designed sampling strategy. (Guisan et al. 2017). Species data sets are spatially aggregated in space. Some habitats being preferentially sampled or outside others being left out based on prior knowledge or the observer’s judgment. (Guisan et al. 2017). Unstructure surveys and sample bias coverage (e.g. along roads).
Effects_Subcategory: Model results often end up mapping the intensity of sampling effort rather than the actual environmental requirements of the species, Inflated statistical significance, Spatial autocorrelation, Misinterpretation of Ecological Processes, Biased Parameter Estimates
Potential_Solutions: Implement target-group background approach (Barber et al. 2021)., Implement sampling probability approach  (Barber et al. 2021)., Implement weighted locality approach  (Barber et al. 2021)., Implement filtering (or thining ) approach (Moudrý et al 2024; Veloz 2009;  Aiello et al. 2015)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: Are there areas where you are concerned about potential bias in the data? Identify areas with potential bias in the data., Are there areas where this species is not sufficiently sampled? Identify areas that are not sufficiently sampled., "To what degree are you concerned about the description and treatment of potential biases in the data, as described in the model metadata?"
Questions_General: Is the available distributional data for the species comprehensively sampled and representative of the regions and environments it currently lives or can potentially live?

Assumption_Description: The assumtption is that species data sets cover the range of the taxon’s environmental tolerances within the study area (Araujo et al 2019). Models attempt to quantify the environmental niche of species through models, and therefore the data used to do so need to include all possible environments that represent suitable habitats for the species modeled (Guisan et al. 2017). See also: Dubos et al. (2022), Taylor et al. (2019), Vollering et al. (2019).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Species data sets (observations, counts, etc.) usually cover a small portion of the range of environmental tolerances within the study area.
Effects_Subcategory: Omission errors
Potential_Solutions: "Implement Ensembles of Small Models (ESMs): Instead of one complex model, generate many simple (bivariate) models and average them to avoid overfitting, which is particularly effective for rare or restricted-range species.", "Implement Spatially Nested Species Distribution Models (N-SDMs): Combine a broad-scale, coarse-grain model (covering the full range) with a local, fine-grain model (focusing on the study area). (Guisan et al. 2025)", Implement Hierarchical Bayesian Models: These models allow for the integration of data from multiple sources and help separate environmental signals from sampling biases., "Implement Environmental Filtering: Instead of filtering by geography, filter the data to ensure an even distribution across the environmental gradients (e.g. Temperature, Elevation) to ensure all tolerances are represented."
Questions_General: How does the chosen geographical extent capture and appropiate environemental extent of the species and influence the definition of the species' ecological niche?, "Does increasing the extent to the entire geographic range provide a more complete picture of the species' environmental tolerances, potentially improving model accuracy by encompassing a wider range of environmental conditions?", "Does a limited extent (e.g. a single island or mountain range) inadvertently truncate the range of environmental conditions the species can tolerate across its full distribution, leading to an incomplete or biased characterization of its niche?", "Alternatively, can too large an extent introduce populations with different environmental responses (due to local adaptation or genetic variation), thereby reducing model accuracy or leading to over-generalized predictions?", "Will a model built on a limited extent (e.g. current conditions) accurately predict potential range shifts or expansions into novel environmental conditions (e.g. under climate change scenarios) where environmental data were not available during model training?", "Does the choice of extent affect the ability to project models to different geographic areas (e.g. predicting an invasive species' potential range in a new region using data only from its native range)?", "Is the chosen extent ecologically relevant to the species' life history, dispersal ability, and habitat selection processes (e.g. home range vs. geographic range)?", "How do different extents and grain sizes affect conclusions made about landscape properties, such as habitat connectivity or the impacts of disturbances like roads?", "Should models be built at a mosaic of extents to account for non-stationarity in species-environment relationships across a broad area, rather than a single, range-wide model?"

Assumption_Description: Samples restricted to and inclusive of all regions of the study area that are suitable for the taxa to establish populations, and that are accessible to the taxon (Araujo et al. 2019). "The geographic extent used to fit the model include all possible conditions that compose the ecological niche of a species" (Guisan et al. 2025).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Species data sets (observations, counts, etc.) usuallly cover a small portion of the regions within the study area. "...there is a risk that the modelled response curves of the species along environmental gradients are truncated, resulting in biased and often incorrect predictions of species distributions" (Chevalier et al. 2021)  (Guisan et al. 2025). "Models built with local species data performed better for the future than those incorporating broad species data and their current predictions reflected the realized distribution of species but they suffered from niche truncation while extrapolated to the future" (Anselmetto et al. 2025).
Effects_Subcategory: Underestimation of the Ecological Niche, It can lead to biased projections of species distributions under climate change
Potential_Solutions: Implement Nested SDMs (N-SDMs; Guisan et al. 2025)., Calibrate models over the whole species range; then focus on regions (e.g.; Scherrer et al.  2021)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: How does the chosen species datasets' geographical extent capture the species' ecological niche?

Assumption_Description: The effective sample size of occurrences is appropriate for the statistical analysis (e.g. Hernandez et al. 2006, Radomski et  al. 2022).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Small sample sizes pose challenges to any statistical analyses and result in decreased predictive potential when compared to models developed with more occurrences (Stockwell and Peterson 2002, McPherson et al. 2004). (e.g. Hernandez et al. 2006).  sample size has the most profound effect on the performance of an SDM (Thibaud et al. 2014, Santini et al. 2021). Sample size poses an important constraint to the model complexity, i.e. to the number of parameters to be estimated, as well as to the algorithms and their settings used for modelling (Moudrý et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Reduce the statistical power
Potential_Solutions: It requires careful consideration of the purpose of the study (Moudrý et al. 2024)., Consider species' ecology: SDMs better predict specialist species with narrow ecological niches than generalist species with wider ecological niches (Tsoar et al. 2007) (Moudrý et al. 2024)., Consider the number of predictors investigated. As the ability to differentiate between influential and non-influential variables decreases with decreasing sample sizes (Moudrý et al. 2024)., Consider the complexity of the shape of species response curves must be taken into account as models based on small sample sizes result in less precise estimates of these shapes (Bazzichetto et al. 2023) (Moudrý et al. 2024)., Consider testing the effect of sample size (Moudrý et al. 2024; see also van Proosdij et al. 2015)., Consider designing accurate SDMs with a well-balanced sampling of as few as 50 presences (Moudrý et al. 2024)., Subsampling; weighting; inputation (see Bowler et al. 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: Is the sample size adequate for both training and robust evaluation of the model?, How does the required sample size change with the species' prevalence (commonness/rarity) and the environmental variability of the study area?, "Will the sample size be sufficient to detect a ""practically"" or ""ecologically"" significant effect, not just a statistically significant one?", "How does sample size impact the choice of modeling technique (e.g. GLM, GAM, Maxent)?", "How was the data collected (sampling method), and does this influence the interpretation of the sample size's adequacy?"

Assumption_Description: The independence of observations refers to the crucial assumption that each recorded species presence (or absence) is a unique, unbiased sample, not influenced by other nearby records or underlying spatial/temporal factors, allowing models to accurately link species to environmental conditions.
Violation_of_Assumptions: When species observations are not independent (e.g. if spatially autocorrelated), the effective number of degrees of freedom used in many statistics associated with the models, e.g. for model selection, no longer corresponds to the apparent number of observations (Legendre, 1993; Legendre et al.2002; Crase et al.2014). Therefore, some statistics may be inaccurate or even wrong (Crase et al.2014)." (Guisan et al. 2017). "Residual spatial autocorrelation can be caused by various factors or processes not accounted for in the modelling, which may include extrinsic processes, such as unknown habitat characteristics (Legendre 1993), intrinsic processes, such as conspecific interactions (Wintle and Bardos 2006), or a combination of the two. (Mielke et al. 2020).
Effects_Subcategory: Inability to capture the true mechanisms driving species distributions, Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Reduced model transferability, Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range expansion, Inaccurate Niche Characterization
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: Does spatial proximity of observations/counts matter?, Do biological processes cause clustering in observations/counts?

Assumption_Description: Multiple assumptions can play a role in population estimates. All individuals within a specific age or stage class have the same probability of survival and reproduction. Every individual in the population has an equal probability of being captured or resighted during a sampling occasion. The population is "closed" to immigration, meaning all changes in population size are accounted for by local births and deaths. All types of data (counts, captures, fecundity) are collected at the same time or within a single, consistent biological season. Survival is assumed to be homogeneous within the year.  (see Plard et al. 2021)
Violation_of_Assumptions: This sampling protocol violates the assumption of recapture homogeneity, i.e. that all females have the same recapture probability. Data were collected during different seasons. Non-immigration. Population model does not include existing non-breeders.
Effects_Subcategory: Misrepresentation of Habitat Needs, Failure to Capture Density Dependence, Reduced model transferability
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "Do the occurrence data used in the model adequately represent the conditions relevant to the species' entire life cycle, including both breeding and non-breeding periods, or are they biased towards a specific season or activity (e.g. ringing data often focuses on breeding or migration)?", "Do the environmental variables used in the model capture seasonal differences in habitat requirements (e.g. specific conditions needed for nesting vs. foraging during non-breeding)?", "Does the spatial resolution of the environmental data match the scale at which the species selects habitat for breeding (e.g. a specific microhabitat for a nest site vs. general climate data)?", "Does the species' environmental niche, particularly its breeding requirements, remain consistent when projecting models to new geographic areas or future climate scenarios, or do they show local adaptation in habitat use?", "Is the species at ""pseudo-equilibrium"" with its current environment across its entire range during the time frame of the study, meaning that all suitable breeding habitats are occupied, and populations aren't rapidly expanding or contracting due to other factors (like dispersal limitation or recent changes)?", "Does the environmental suitability predicted by the model actually equate to high-quality habitat that supports high reproductive success and population growth, or just transient presence?", "Can the model predictions be related to actual population parameters like survival and reproduction rates, which ultimately determine the species' persistence, or do they merely indicate probability of presence?", "Do the models distinguish between ""source"" habitats (where reproduction exceeds mortality, producing a population surplus) and ""sink"" habitats (where mortality exceeds reproduction, and populations are only maintained by immigration), which often rely on linking SDM outputs to demographic parameters?"

Assumption_Description: It is often assumed that if a species is present at a sampled site, it will be detected. In reality, detection can be imperfect due to survey conditions or species behavior.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Failure to account for imperfect detection leads to classifying true presences as false absences, which can bias the model's estimate of habitat suitability and limit its ability to identify true presence sites accurately (e.g. Lahoz et al. 2013).
Effects_Subcategory: Omission errors
Potential_Solutions: Consider using occupancy models (e.g. Erickson & Smith 2021; Comte & Grenoulliet 2013)., Consider data integration and fusion., Consider using advance modelling frameworks such as Joint Species Dsitribution Models (JSDMs).
Questions_General: Does the failure to detect a species when it is present (false absence error) lead to a significant underestimation of its true distribution and occurrence probability?, "Does imperfect detection bias the assumed relationships between a species and its environment, potentially leading to incorrect conclusions about its habitat requirements?", "Can models based on the perfect detection assumption lead to mischaracterization of conservation problems (e.g. classifying a difficult-to-detect species as critically endangered or extinct when it is not) and inefficient management decisions? ", "Does the probability of detection change across different locations, times of day, or seasons, and how does this variation affect the model's performance?", "Are there specific environmental or survey-related variables (e.g. observer experience, weather, habitat density) that influence detectability, and how can these be effectively incorporated into models to correct the bias?", "Does accounting for imperfect detection require specialized and often more resource-intensive data collection methods (e.g. multiple surveys per site, double-observer data, mark-recapture methods), which may not be feasible for the model application?", Do models that incorporate detection probability introduce additional complexity and potential difficulties in model fitting and interpretation?, "For a large proportion of species, especially less-studied taxa like plants and invertebrates, is there a complete lack of information on their detectability, making it challenging to adjust models accordingly?", "By explicitly accounting for imperfect detection, how much does the reliability and predictive accuracy of the species distribution maps actually improve, compared to simpler ""naïve"" models?", Which model evaluation metrics are appropriate when detection is imperfect? Standard metrics like AUC (Area Under the Curve) can be misleading if they assess the ability to predict detections rather than actual occurrences?

Criterion: Predictors are adequate in spatial and temporal resolution
Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: Spatial resolution of predictors are adequate to capture species habitat requirements, distributions, etc. (Soley-Guardia et al. (2024) and Araujo et al. (2019). See also, Šímová et al. 2019, Rödder & Engler 2011, Bradter et al. 2012, Chauvier et al. 2022, Nadeau et al. 2016.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Mismatches in spatiotemporal scales between species and covariates datasets (e.g. Wiens et al. 2010).
Effects_Subcategory: It can add residual spatial autocorrelation (RSA), Decrease variance explained, Some uncertainties may be averaged out if the grain size of predicted distributions is large, Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Inaccurate Niche Characterization
Potential_Solutions: Identify appropriate scales of predictors (e.g. Bradter et al. 2012)., Test relevant covariates at multiple scales.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: To what degree are you concerned about the spatial resolution of the predictor variables?
Questions_General: At what spatial and temporal scales are the covariates appropriate?, Is the spatial accuracy of the predictor variables appropriate for the scale of the species' occurrence data?

Assumption_Description: Some variables exert a primary control on the species distributions and suitable habitat. (Araujo et al. 2019):
Violation_of_Assumptions: Key predictors are not included in the model
Effects_Subcategory: Reduce the statistical power, Misidentification of key environmental drivers, Underestimation of the Ecological Niche
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption., "Modelers might consider this criterion is irrelevant or has minimum impact on the accuracy of model precictions. Modelers are looking only for high model accuracy and low uncertainty, regarless of explaining of predictors."
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: "Are you concerned about the description and treatment of predictor data, as described in the model metadata.", How concerned are you about missing predictors?, What predictors are missing and why?, How concerned are you about superfluous or inappropriate predictors?, Which predictors should be removed and why?, How concerned are you about inaccuracy in the model predictors?, "If so, describe the predictor(s) and the areas of concern."

Criterion: Predictors are relevant for species distribution/abundances
Author_reference: Araujo et al. (2019), Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Assumption_Description: Variables included in the models do in fact reflect the niche requirements of a species" (Wiens et al. 2009). "All important environmental predictors required to capture the desired niche of the modeled species are assumed to be available at the resolution relevant for the organism being modeled (Guisan et al. 2017).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Unmeasured niche dimensions
Effects_Subcategory: Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Potentially misrepresenting suitable habitats, Underprediciton of potential regions of suitability, Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range expansion, Reduced model transferability, Overestimation of suitable habitat, Understimate range contraction, Underestimation of the Ecological Niche
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption., "Modelers might consider this criterion is irrelevant or has minimum impact on the accuracy of model precictions. Modelers are looking only for high model accuracy and low uncertainty, regarless of explaining of predictors."

Assumption_Description: Predictors measured without error (Araujo et al. 2019):. This issue is rarely assessed in studies of SDMs, although errors are an inherent factor in each GIS predictor layer (McInerny and Purves, Reference McInerny and Purves2011; Marion et al., 2012). Although it is essentially impossible to guarantee zero errors in mapped environmental predictors, estimates of spatial distribution of errors could be associated with each layer and used to calculate spatial uncertainty in the model predictions (Barry and Elith 2006; Van Niel and Austin 2007). There is still a need for a proper method for combining errors from the different environmental variables in the model. This might be achieved using Bayesian approaches that can account for prior knowledge and uncertainty in environmental layers (McInerny and Purves 2011; Keil et al. 2014). (Guisan et al. 2017).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Environmental layer (e.g. climate variables) with error and spatial uncertainty not considered in modelling
Effects_Subcategory: Reduce the statistical power, Erros in model intepretation, Biased Parameter Estimates, Commission errors, Omission errors
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: How concerned are you about inaccuracy in the model predictors?

Assumption_Description: SDMs assummes future scenarios are within the range of known enviromental conditions (Araujo et al. 2019). That is, climatic conditions between training and introduced areas are analogous (Richmond et al. 2010)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Novel climatic conditions might occur in introduced area (Richmond et al. 2010). Modeled responses extrapolate beyond range of values for environmental predicitors found in native range (Richmond et al. 2010).
Effects_Subcategory: Biased Parameter Estimates, Omission errors, Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range expansion, Understimate range contraction, Potentially misrepresenting suitable habitats, Underprediciton of potential regions of suitability
Potential_Solutions: Use a clamping procedure to limit predictions in regions with novel climatic conditions., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "Does projecting models into novel environmental space (environments not represented in the current data) introduce rising, unquantifiable uncertainty, especially in areas expected to undergo significant climate change?", "Is there a limit to the forecast horizon (e.g. are the first 30 years of projections more reliable than long-term ones), after which novelty makes them unreliable?", Does the species' niche remain fixed (niche conservatism)?

Criterion: Models or scenarios (climate, land use and land cover, etc.) to calculate predictors
Assumption_Description: SDMs assummes that models and scenarios of climate change & LULCC represent what is on the ground (e.g. Global circulation models-GCMs or  RCPs). See McMahon et al 2015, Cheaib et al. 2012, Thomas et al. 2004,  Thuiller et al. 2004)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Global Circulation Models (GCMs) rely on multiple parameters, assumptions and downscaling and interpolation approaches that might add uncertainty to SDMs.
Effects_Subcategory: Overstimate range expansion, Understimate range expansion, Biased estimates of range dynamics, It can lead to biased projections of species distributions under climate change
Potential_Solutions: "Use an ensemble of multiple GCMs and different Representative Concentration Pathways (RCPs, e.g. RCP 4.5, RCP 8.5) to project a range of potential future conditions.", "Dynamical downscaling uses regional climate models nested within GCMs to simulate climate processes at a finer scale, which can better simulate non-linear changes.", Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: Which abiotic and biotic variables should be included (and which excluded)?, At what spatial and temporal scales are the covariates appropriate?, Are the assumed species-environment relationships realistic?, "What is the quality and spatial/temporal resolution of the future climate data (e.g. General Circulation Models, GCMs)?", Is the spatial accuracy of the predictor variables appropriate for the scale of the species' occurrence data?

Criterion: Model complexity and structure is appropriate
Assumption_Description: The choice of model complexity (Araujo et al. 2019); e.g. linear vs. non-linear, single vs. multiple factors) can affect model accuracy and robustness (e.g. Brun et al. 2019), Merow et al. 2014).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Simplifications made in the formulation, structure and complexity of the model are inapropriate
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Potentially misrepresenting suitable habitats
Potential_Solutions: Comparing a range of models (predictive performance) & response shapes (Ramampiandra et al. 2023)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: What other concerns or comments do you have about model complexity?

Criterion: Treatment of bias and noise in species datasets
Assumption_Description: There is no geographical and environmental bias in species data sets (Araujo et al. 2019):, and its treatment is irrelevant
Violation_of_Assumptions: Treatment of bias and noise in species datasets is not appropriate or does not correct address the issue
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors
Potential_Solutions: Spatial thining/filtering., Background/Pseudo-absence Point Manipulation approach., Implement Integrated Species Distribution Models (ISDMs) approach that combines data from multiple sources., Applying weights to observations can account for varying sampling reliability or effort., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "How does the species' ecology (e.g. rarity, specialization, social behavior) interact with survey methods to introduce bias into population estimates?", "To what extent can data gaps and biases be treated as a ""missing data problem"" (e.g. Missing At Random vs. Missing Not At Random), and what are the implications of making such assumptions?", "Are the methods for mitigating different types of bias (e.g. geographic thinning, environmental filtering, or weighting background points) effective?", "Is data quantity or spatial evenness more important for improving model predictions, and should researchers prioritize collecting more data or investing in more spatially balanced sampling?"

Assumption_Description: There is no collinearity among predictors (Araujo et al. 2019).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Treatment of collinearity in predictors  is not appropriate or does not correct address the issue (Dormann et al. 2012).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors
Potential_Solutions: The simplest approach is to identify highly correlated variables (often using a correlation matrix or the Variance Inflation Factor (VIF)) and remove one from each highly correlated pair., "Implement dimensionality reduction techniques (PCAs Partial least squares, etc.).", "Algorithm solutions (regularization techniques, type of algorithm, etc.).", Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Criterion: Dealing with modelling and parameter uncertainty
Assumption_Description: There multiple sources of model uncertainty that modellers should acknowledge (Araujo et al. 2019).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Multiple source of uncertaintynot addressed in model fitting: species datsets, covariates, model overparametrization, algorithms, initial conditions, rare events, stochasticity, etc.
Effects_Subcategory: Biased Parameter Estimates, Reduced model transferability
Potential_Solutions: Spend effort on reducing SDM extrapolation rather than improving model fit (e.g. fitting species data over a broader range of environmental conditions). (Brodie et al. 2022)., Identify which uncertainties dominate your predictions (Dietze et al. 2018)., Quantify uncertainty in model predictions (e.g. Thomas et al. 2024)., Validate predictions using new data sets., Using a combination of multiple diverging earth system models and multiple types or parameterizations of SDMs will effectively capture uncertainty. (Brodie et al. 2022)., Quantify uncertainty over time; as SDM uncertainty can dominate at longer time horizons (>40 years); and decadal variability can drive nondirectional trends in uncertainty. (Brodie et al. 2022). See also  (e.g. Thomas et al. 2024; Morley et al. 2020)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Assumption_Description: The algorithm selected is the "best" option given the data sets (species and predictors). See Lawler et al. 2006, Shabani et al. 2016,
Violation_of_Assumptions: Algoritnm choice does not match species occurrence data requiemerents (see Wiens et al. 2009).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Potentially misrepresenting suitable habitats, Reduce the statistical power, Biased Parameter Estimates
Potential_Solutions: Consider ensemble models., Assess model performance; using Tru Skill Statistics (e.g. Shabani et al. 2016; Allouche et al. 2006)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: How concerned are you about the appropriateness of the modeling algorithm for the model objectives and application?

Assumption_Description: Training samples are representative of the enviromental conditions in native range. (Richmond et al. 2010)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Poor or lack of independent training datasets
Effects_Subcategory: Omission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Underprediciton of potential regions of suitability
Potential_Solutions: Generate adequate number of random pseudo-absences points from native range., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Assumption_Description: Quatitative approaches are appropriate for model goals, data, etc. Different types of response variables require different types of statistical models (Guisan and Zimmermannn2000). For instance, semi-quantitative data require very specific modeling techniques, such as ordinal regressions (Guisan and Harrell2000). Quantitative data are easier to model and there are numerous techniques available for doing so (see Part III) but, different types of quantitative responses will still require different types of statistical models. Counts of species or of individuals usually require specifying Poisson, negative binomial, or other probability distribution functions (PDF) for discrete positive values, a requirement that can be met using several modeling techniques, such as generalized regressions (e.g. Vincent and Haworth1983) or boosted regression trees (e.g. Thuiller et al.2006; see Part III). Binary response variables, such as species’ presence–absence data, require binomial PDF and logistic transformation (e.g. the link function in GLMs and generalized additive models (GAM); see Part III) and represent by far the data for which the largest range of modeling techniques is available in ready-to-use packages. Techniques for binary response variables are also the main ones presented in this book. Once a statistical method has been chosen, it is assumed to be the right one for the data in hand. Failure to identify the correct method can lead to errors and uncertainty in the predictions (e.g. Guisan et al.2002). (Guisan et al. 2017)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Using default settings in model parametrization
Effects_Subcategory: Reduce the statistical power, Biased Parameter Estimates
Potential_Solutions: Evaluate a set of candidate models using independent data (if available) or robust cross-validation methods to assess their fit and predictive power., Manage model complexity: Overly complex models with many parameters can overfit the data and perform poorly when extrapolating to new conditions. Balance complexity with data availability to ensure reliable predictions.

Criterion: Shape of the species and covariates relationships
Assumption_Description: Species responses to environmental factors are often non-linear (e.g. a bell-shaped curve showing optimal conditions rather than a straight line)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Use of modelling approaches that do not handle non-linear relationships
Effects_Subcategory: Reduce the statistical power, Biased Parameter Estimates, It can add residual spatial autocorrelation (RSA)
Potential_Solutions: Transform covariates., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: What interactions or nonlinear relationships are missing?
Questions_General: Are the assumed species-environment relationships realistic?

Criterion: Correlative relationships (species-enviromnment) discovered are stationary over space
Assumption_Description: "Broad-scale ecological research on species distributions commonly presumes that the correlative relationships discovered are stationary over space" (Pease et al. 2022).
Violation_of_Assumptions: "..as the spatial extent of a modeling increases, the likelihood of heterogeneity in the response and the resulting species–environment relationships varying across space also increases. That is, a fixed set of regression coefficients estimated in a species distribution model (SDM) may not represent the actual space-varying relationships between a response (e.g. animal abundance or occupancy) and environmental covariates (e.g. forest cover) (Figure 1). This is termed “nonstationarity” and describes variation in the magnitude and/or direction of a correlative relationship across space or time (Rollinson et al., 2021)" (Pease et al. 2022).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Inability to capture the true mechanisms driving species distributions, The relationship between a species and its environment might not be consistent across space (ecological non-stationarity), Redue model transferability
Potential_Solutions: Geographically Weighted Regression (GWR) Models., Spatiotemporal variability in the R package sdmTMB (Ward et al. 2022)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: "How reliable are future species distribution projections if the underlying relationships between species and their environment (ecological niches) are assumed to be static (stationary) but are, in fact, changing over time or space due to climate change or other factors?", "To what extent does violating the non-stationarity assumption introduce bias and uncertainty into model outputs, and for which species or situations is this bias most significant?", "What are the most effective methods (e.g. breakpoint analysis, moving window analysis, statistical tests for stationarity) for identifying the presence and nature of non-stationarity in ecological data?", How can models be formulated to incorporate non-stationarity?, "Should researchers use local approaches where model coefficients vary across space, or use dynamic/process-based models that explicitly account for changing processes over time?", How does the choice of predictor variables affect the detection and impact of non-stationarity?, "At what spatial and temporal scales is the stationarity assumption a valid approximation, and at what scales does it become problematic for different research questions (e.g. short-term, local studies vs. long-term, broad-scale climate change impacts)?"

Assumption_Description: Model performance metrics are appropriate for model assessment.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Computing AUC and TSS statistics requires the use of true absences (Guillaumot et al. 2019). When the potential distribution is the goal of the research, the AUC is not an appropriate performance measure because the weight of commission errors is much lower than that of omission errors. (Jiménez-Valverde 2011)
Effects_Subcategory: Inadequate model selection, Reduced model transferability
Potential_Solutions: "Selecting the Right Metrics: Different metrics (AUC, TSS, Kappa, Tjur's R2), etc.) evaluate different aspects of model performance (e.g. discrimination, calibration, accuracy) and respond differently to factors like species prevalence.", "Consider Ecological Plausibility: Do not just focus on a high performance score; ensure that the model's relationships with environmental variables are ecologically relevant and make sense based on existing knowledge of the species (e.g. expected temperature responses).", Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: How concerned are you about the adequacy or appropriateness of the validation metrics?, What are the metrics that should be used and why?

Assumption_Description: Models are not sensitive to different background point selection strategies (e.g. Steen et al. 2024).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Selecting random points when there is high sampling bias results in a model assuming that areas beyond the areas sampled heavily are in fact absence locations which is often untrue, and your resulting model will predict your species occurs in areas you sampled but will often miss areas where the species occurs but were not sampled adequately.
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Reduced model transferability, Biased Parameter Estimates, Misidentification of key environmental drivers
Potential_Solutions: Assess predcitive performance; using  different types of background selections (Steen et al. 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Assumption_Description: Models are fairly insensitive to settings that affect their complexity; or default settings result in an acceptable parameterization regardless of biases and nuances of each input dataset.... This is especially problematic when the occurrence data are biased ..." (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024). See also, Hallgren et al. 2019, ).
Violation_of_Assumptions: The configuration settings in SDMs is not standard and alternative to default values must be pursue.
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Potentially misrepresenting suitable habitats, Underprediciton of potential regions of suitability, Reduced model transferability
Potential_Solutions: Assess multiple parameterization scenarios (Soley et al. 2024; Valavi et al. 2021)., Document and explain model settings (Hallgren et al. 2019)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Criterion: Reducing predictor variables irrespective of their information content
Assumption_Description: "Modeling with fewer or less correlated variables yields simpler and better models (Breiman 2001, Elith et al. 2008, Evans et al. 2013) that are not fitted to nuances in the data" (Soley-Guardia et al. (2024)
Violation_of_Assumptions: There is a potential situation where two or more predictor variables in a statistical model are linearly related. However, removing predictor variables a priori without consideration of their information content may arbitrarily discard informative data (reducing the predictive ability of the model) and does not directly address the problem of overfitting (Breiman 2001, Olden et al. 2008, Braunisch et al. 2013) (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Omission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions, Reduce the statistical power, It inflates the variance of regression parameters, Reduced model transferability, Misidentification of key environmental drivers
Potential_Solutions: Assess spatial autocorrelation in model residuals., Use spatial autoregressive models., Use spatial eigenvector mapping., Remove collinear variables to reduce redundancy., Reduce dimenssiionality (Zhang et al. 2023)., Orthogonal Principal Componenents (Farrel et al. 2019)., Algorithms might account for redundant variables (e.g.; Feng et al. 2019)., Select environmental variables for groups of species with similar natural histories (Soley et al. 2024) ., Variable selection approach (Cobos et al. 2019)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: Which predictors should be removed and why?, Which predictors would you expect to be more or less important and why?

Criterion: Using an overly large study region
Assumption_Description: "Within the study region for model training, the species occurs in spatial equilibrium (i.e., the species inhabits all suitable areas) or environmental equilibrium (i.e., although the species does not occur in all suitable areas, it inhabits an environmentally representative (unbiased) subset of them)".  A"n appropriate study region approximating the assumption of spatial equilibrium (or the noise assumptions) should be defined using available information regarding the species' natural history and the configuration of the landscape". (Soley et al. 2024)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Often, researchers use an overly large study region for model building and/or evaluation (Araújo and Peterson 2012, Liang et al. 2018). Sometimes such a region is selected with the misconception that it will yield an accurate depiction of the species' range (rather than the areas suitable for it) but probably most often because a map of suitability is desired in that full area. (Soley et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Commission errors, Potentially misrepresenting species distributions
Potential_Solutions: "Avoid artificial boundaries and utilize ecological classifications (e.g. ecoregions, biomes, etc.).", Use Spatially Nested SDMs (N-SDMs)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.
Questions_General: What is the trade-off between resolution and extent?, "Does a limited extent (e.g. a single island or mountain range) inadvertently truncate the range of environmental conditions the species can tolerate across its full distribution, leading to an incomplete or biased characterization of its niche?", "Does increasing the extent to the entire geographic range provide a more complete picture of the species' environmental tolerances, potentially improving model accuracy by encompassing a wider range of environmental conditions?", "Alternatively, can too large an extent introduce populations with different environmental responses (due to local adaptation or genetic variation), thereby reducing model accuracy or leading to over-generalized predictions?", "Will a model built on a limited extent (e.g. current conditions) accurately predict potential range shifts or expansions into novel environmental conditions (e.g. under climate change scenarios) where environmental data were not available during model training?", "Does the choice of extent affect the ability to project models to different geographic areas (e.g. predicting an invasive species' potential range in a new region using data only from its native range)?", "Is the chosen extent ecologically relevant to the species' life history, dispersal ability, and habitat selection processes (e.g. home range vs. geographic range)?", "How do different extents and grain sizes affect conclusions made about landscape properties, such as habitat connectivity or the impacts of disturbances like roads?", "Should models be built at a mosaic of extents to account for non-stationarity in species-environment relationships across a broad area, rather than a single, range-wide model?", How does niche truncation bias predictions?, When is niche truncation a problem?, How does the shape of the truncated response curve influence outcomes?

Assumption_Description: Error rates for omission (false negatives) and commission (false positives) are both accurately calculated. For commission error and metrics that include it, comparisons across species or regions assume that the ratio of suitable to unsuitable environments remains constant (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Sampling bias and lack of data affect model performance. Critically, it follows that because of this bias, such metrics do not represent values of absolute performance, but instead relative ones valid for comparisons only with other analyses for the same species and study region (Soley-Guardia et a. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: It limits the identification of optimal threshold for converting predcitions to binary output
Potential_Solutions: Implement null models (Bohl et al. 2019)., Validation omission rate (Soley et al 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Criterion: Model training and validation datasets and approaches are appropiate
Assumption_Description: Training and validation datasets created using random splits are independent from one another (Soleye t al. 2024). The method used for valiadation is the most appropiate for evaluating model reliability (e.g. resubtitution, data-splitting, independant data (e.g. Araujo et al. 2005, Hintze et al. 2021, Finn et al. 2024)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Predicitve accuracy of SDMs can only be fully tested by means of validation studies using direct comparison of model predictions with independent. (Araujo et al. 2005). Training and validation datasets created using random splits are independent from one another (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Undesired fitting to biases in the training data which is more likely in overly complex models- will go undetected – yielding inflated measures of performance
Potential_Solutions: Splitting data non-randomly  (Soley et al. 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Criterion: Misunderstanding or overlooking the effects of extrapolation
Assumption_Description: The modeled response to a given variable continues its trend unconstrained.  It is assumed that the response remains fixed at the last value of the training dataset Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Violation_of_Assumptions: The new conditions typically contain at least some non-analog environments (beyond those found in the training study region). This makes extrapolation of the modeled response curves necessary to make a prediction (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Reduced model transferability
Potential_Solutions: Assessing suitability curves (Owen et al. 2013)., Identify areas of extrict extrapolation; using index such as MESS and MOP (See Owen et al. 2013)., Flagging areas with high uncertainty due to extrapolation (Soley et al. 2024)., Assess model's robustness to extrapolation (e.g.; Roberts et al. 2016)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Assumption_Description: Models are able to accurately predict species responses under novel conditions. (See Charney et al. 2021).
Violation_of_Assumptions: There will be novel conditions in some geographic areas under climate change scenarios that are not considered.
Effects_Subcategory: The relationship between a species and its environment might not be consistent across space (ecological non-stationarity)
Potential_Solutions: Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Criterion: Comparing model outputs across different species or places
Assumption_Description: Values given to scaling parameters are appropriate...........  the rescaling parameters are still equivalent among the entities involved (e.g. for ecologically similar species), rendering output values that are consistently incorrect yet comparable in absolute terms (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Violation_of_Assumptions: Suitability can only be interpreted as relative across the study area of a given analysis, and the values of the predictions of different models should not be directly compared across species or regions (Soley-Guardia et al. 2024).
Effects_Subcategory: Suitability can only be interpreted as relative across the study area of a given analysis, The values of the predictions of different models should not be directly compared across species or regions
Potential_Solutions: Estimates of occupancy or abundance can be used to set the scaling parameters rather than relying on default values (Soley et al . 2024)., Make assumptions regarding scaling parameters for sets of species with similar natural histories (Soley et al . 2024)., Acknowledge modelers were unable to address this assumption.

Assumption_Description: Demonstrated lack of violation of, or robustness to, assumptions relevant for technique being used. Or consequencesfor inteppretation of results.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Most relevant assumptions (e.g. fundamental) and their potential effect on model rpedcitions (e.g. Very high risk in commision errors) are violated (e.g. Wiens et al. 2009).
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: To what degree are you concerned about how well the modelling framework addresses important statistical assumptions?
Questions_General: "What is the relative influence of sampling bias, sample size, and positional uncertainty on model results, and which factor is most important to address?"

Assumption_Description: Model is robust, performance metrics are meaningful, predictions are accurate and generalizable.
Violation_of_Assumptions: Overall assessment from the modelers perspective is that model is not quite robust and might mislead potenial usres
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: Identify the areas on the map where predictions are inaccurate., To what degree are you concerned about the spatial resolution of the predictor variables?
Questions_General: "Does a high model performance (e.g. accuracy) guarantee high reliability in the ecological relationships or transferability of predictions to different areas or future climates?"

Assumption_Description: Measures of performance exhaustively summarize goodness of fit and discrimination. And, Patterns of uncertainty comprehensively characterized
Violation_of_Assumptions: Uncertainty is a generalized in model predcitions, so modelers might advise use the model predictions with high caution
Questions_Evaluation_Tool: What other concerns do you have about model performance?, "Identify areas on the map where uncertainty does not align with your understanding of data limitations, known habitat complexities, or other sources of uncertainty."

Assumption_Description: Transforming continuous habitat suitability predictions into a binary (presence/absence) map requires a threshold. The choice of this threshold directly influences the balance between omission and commission errors. (e.g. Benito et al. 2013)
Violation_of_Assumptions: Binary maps are highly sensitive to threshold's values (e.g. Abdel-Moniem et al. 2025).
Potential_Solutions: Compare multiple thresholding methods (see Abdel-Moniem et al. 2025; Guisan et al. (2025).
